segments.s489-g57: گر introduces a conditional ('if'). از + گنج marks the object 'ask of/about the treasure' and پرسی is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person present form 'you ask' (used here for a hypothetical). خود اندازه نیست literally places 'خود' (itself) before 'اندازه' (measure/size) with the negator نیست, so the clause means that the treasure 'has no measure' or is unmeasurable; the compressed order and lack of an ezafe are typical of poetic diction.
segments.s489-g58: سخنهای او = 'his words/sayings' with the plural suffix attached to سخن and the pronoun او following (poetic word order without an explicit ezafe). در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world', and تازه نیست is a present negative copula meaning 'is not new' — together: 'his words are not new in the world' (i.e., not novel).
segments.s489-g17: Literal word order: subject (سکندر) + prepositional phrase (به دیدار او) + verb (گشت) + predicate adjective (شاد). گشت here is the past/resultative 'became' (classical/poetic use of گشت = شد). به دیدار او = 'at/for the meeting with him' (به + noun phrase) — functionally 'on seeing him' or 'at the meeting'.
segments.s489-g18: همان 'that same/that one' points back to a previously mentioned person; گفت is the verb 'said' (the provided noun-senses don't include the verbal sense, so marked -1 below). The clause بدخواه او گشت باد is an optative/wish: گشت (become) + باد (optative particle 'may it be') = 'may he become ...'. بدخواه او = 'his ill-wisher' / 'one who wishes him ill' (word order: predicate noun before the optative verb).
segments.s489-g19: Standard locative expression: به مصر اندرون بود = 'was inside Egypt' (اندرون 'within/inside'); بود is the past copula 'was'. یک سال modifies duration 'for one year'; شاه is the subject 'the king'. Word order is somewhat flexible/poetic (temporal/directional phrases placed before the verb).
segments.s489-g20: بدان here is the fused/poetic 'به + آن' (roughly 'with that / for that'); تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'until'). برآسود is a past/resultative verbal form meaning 'was/became at rest, relieved'. شاه و سپاه are the coordinated beneficiaries/subjects of that result. In modern terms: 'for that, so that the king and army might rest.'
segments.s489-g21: Existential construction: زنی بود 'there was a woman'; در اندلس gives the location 'in Andalus'; شهریار functions as a predicate nominative 'a ruler/sovereign' — in classical Persian a feminine ruler can still be called شهریار in poetic usage. بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s489-g22: Two coordinated descriptions: خردمند 'wise' (adjective predicated of the woman) and با لشکری بی‌شمار 'with an army innumerable' (با + noun phrase meaning 'having/with an army that is countless'). Word order places the adjective and the with-phrase after the head noun, typical in Persian.
segments.s489-g23: Three-word nominal clause: جهانجوی 'world-seeking/ambitious' and بخشنده 'generous' are epithets modifying the proper name قیدافه, followed by بود 'was'. In classical Persian epithets frequently precede the proper name; the whole phrase states identity/qualities of the person.
segments.s489-g24: ز (poetic 'from'/'because of') + روی بهی 'a fair/beautiful face' is the source/cause; یافته is a perfect/past participial verb 'having acquired/obtained'; کام و سود are the two direct objects 'desire (wish/satisfaction) and benefit/profit'. Word order: source ('ز روی بهی') precedes the verb phrase 'یافته کام و سود'.
segments.s489-g9: جهانگیر is the proper name (subject). با introduces an accompaniment ('with') and لشکری is an indefinite, attributive noun 'an army/troops' (لشکری = لشکر + ی). راه‌جوی is a postpositive adjective/epithet ('road-seeking' = travelling/on the march) modifying the army; Persian often stacks such modifiers after the noun in poetic style.
segments.s489-g10: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. جده is the place-name Jeddah. سوی + مصر expresses direction 'toward Egypt'. بنهاد is a past tense of نهادن (here 'he set/placed/turned'); in classical/poetic Persian the prefixed بـ appears in some past forms. روی is the noun 'face' used in the idiom روی نهادن = 'to set/turn (one's) face toward'. Word order is literal: 'from Jeddah toward Egypt he set (his) face'.
segments.s489-g11: This is a nominal clause with the copula بود ('was'). ملک here functions as 'sovereign/king' (not 'realm') and قیطون is the ruler's name. به مصر اندرون is a locative phrase 'in the interior of Egypt' (به + place + اندرون = within). The order 'ملک بود [name] به [place] اندرون' is typical of epic diction where the copula follows the predicate.
segments.s489-g12: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic pronoun -ش ('his army'). ز here is the poetic 'from/of' used in comparisons/phrases; راه is 'road' and گمانی ('by conjecture/appearance') qualifies the following adjective فزون 'greater/more numerous'. The clause is verbless: noun + qualifiers + adjective, reading 'his army, in apparent number on the road, was greater.'
segments.s489-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is 3rd-person past 'he heard'. کامد is an archaic/present-form verb meaning 'he comes/came' (common in epic diction); together چو + past + کامد gives a sequence 'when he heard, he came'. ز راه حرم is a prepositional phrase 'from/along the road of the sanctuary' (حرم = sacred precinct or inner quarters).
segments.s489-g14: جهانگیر is the subject again, followed by the epithet پیروز 'victorious' (postpositive adjective). با introduces the manner 'with' and باد و دم 'wind and breath' is an idiomatic pair meaning 'swiftly/with haste' (literally 'with wind and breath'). This short clause gives the manner of his arrival.
segments.s489-g15: پذیره شدش is a passive/periphrastic expression 'he was received' or 'it befell him to be welcomed' (پذیره from پذیرَش/پذیرفتن + شد + enclitic ش = 'him'). با فراوان سپاه is an instrumental/accompanying phrase 'with a plentiful army' (فراوان modifies سپاه). The construction is a typical passive reception formula in epic poetry.
segments.s489-g16: This is an asyndetic enumeration introduced to show what accompanied the reception. The first item (ابا) is uncertain/corrupt in isolation (likely a contracted poetic form such as او با 'he/they with'), so its precise dictionary sense is unclear here. The following nouns form a list of gifts/tributes: بدره 'full‑moon' (a poetic epithet for beauties), برده 'slave/captive', تاج 'crown', and گاه (here as 'store/occasion/treasure' in poetic listing). Conjunctions (و) simply join the items.
segments.s489-g41: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when' introducing a past-time clause. قیدافه is a proper name (the clause's subject). چهر سکندر is a genitive phrase 'the face of S.' with possessor after the head noun (چهر + سکندر). بدید is the past (preterite) form 'saw' (older/poetic form with the b- prefix).
segments.s489-g42: غمی گشت is a light verbal phrase meaning 'grief came' or 'became sorrowful' (غمی = grief as subject/experiential noun; گشت = 'became/turned'). The coordinated و links the next two actions. بنهفت here appears in the text as an opaque item; grammatically it behaves as a coordinated predicate with گشت (i.e. a following action 'fell/sat'), though the lexicon entry given is a proper name and the exact meaning is uncertain in the manuscript. دم در کشید is a compound verbal chunk: دم 'breath' + در 'door' (here 'at the doorway') + کشید 'drew' (past of کشیدن), an idiom meaning 'to draw a breath' or 'to sigh' at the door; کشید is the main past verb of this small clause.
segments.s489-g43: سکندر is the subject. ز + قیطون here marks the addressee/ source 'of/from Qeytun' (classical use of ز = 'from/of' when asking someone). بپرسید is the past tense 'asked' (poetic preterite with b-), and the coordinating و connects that speech act to گفت 'and said' (reporting verb).
segments.s489-g44: که introduces an indirect question ('who/which'). قیدافه را uses the definite-object marker را to mark the person under discussion. بر زمین is an adverbial phrase 'on the earth' (= 'in the world'). کیست is an interrogative copula built from کی 'who' + ست 'is' (inverted order typical of Persian questions). جفت is the predicate noun 'mate/match' — the clause asks who is Qeydāfeh's match on earth.
segments.s489-g45: بدو is a prepositional/adverbial form 'to him / unto him' marking the indirect object of گفت. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (classical past). قیطون is the speaker. که‌ای شهریار is a vocative phrase: که‌ای (classical/poetic form akin to که ای) = 'O', addressing شهریار 'king'.
segments.s489-g46: چنو is a demonstrative/qualifier 'such/so' (here 'no one such as'); نیست is the negative copula 'is not'. اندر جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world' (اندر = in/within; placed before the predicate by poetic word order). کامگار is the predicate adjective 'successful' or 'fortunate' — together meaning 'there is no one so successful in the world.'
segments.s489-g47: شمار سپاهش is a genitive phrase 'the number of his army' (سپاهش = سپاه + suffix ش 'his'). نداند is the negative present/past form 'knows not' (classical negation plus دانستن). کسی follows the verb in a negative statement and functions as an indefinite/negative subject meaning 'no one' or 'anyone' depending on context; here it yields 'no one knows.'
segments.s489-g48: مگر introduces an exception 'except / unless'. باز جوید is a verbal phrase 'seeks again' (باز = again; جوید is the classical present/subjunctive form 'seeks/searches' used here to mark the conditional/exception). ز دفتر = 'from the book(s)/records' (ز = from; دفتر = book/record). بسی is an intensifier meaning 'much/many' (classical usage 'much' or 'widely'); the whole clause means 'unless someone again searches widely in the records.'
segments.s489-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برگشت و آمد are two past actions compressed together: برگشت 'returned' and آمد 'came', with the subject omitted (pro-drop). به درگاه قصر is a prepositional phrase 'to the court of the palace' (درگاه قصر = 'royal court/threshold of the palace').
segments.s489-g2: ببخشید is the past form of بخشیدن used here as 'bestowed/gave'. The verb appears before its objects (poetic word order). دینار چندی = 'several/some dinars' (چندی after the noun = an indefinite quantity). به نصر marks the recipient 'to Nasr' (proper name).
segments.s489-g3: توانگر شد is a simple past 'became wealthy' (توانگر = 'wealthy', شد = 'became'). آنکس که introduces a relative clause 'the one who…', with که linking to درویش بود 'was a dervish' (بود = past copula). Word order places predicate then the nominal relative phrase (poetic inversion).
segments.s489-g4: وگر is a conditional 'if / but if' introducing the hypothetical. خوردش is خورد + ـش (the past of خوردن used idiomatically here 'befall/come to' = 'be acquired by him'), with ـش marking the object 'him/it'. از کوشش خویش is an ablative phrase 'from/of his effort' (خویش = 'his own', formed here as خوی + ش). Final بود is the past copula 'was', yielding 'if it came to him by effort, it was by his own effort.'
segments.s489-g5: وزان = 'and from that' (linking adverbial). جایگه here = 'place' (from that place). شاد لشکر = 'the joyful army' (adjective before noun). براند is the narrative verb 'set forth / drove (on)' placed at the end (classical/poetic verb-final order).
segments.s489-g6: به جده درآمد is a verb phrase 'they entered / arrived at Jeddah' (به + place, درآمد from درآمدن 'to enter/come in'). فراوان نماند literally 'much did not remain' = 'not much remained' (فراوان 'much/abundant' with the negative verb نماند 'was not left').
segments.s489-g7: سپه را is the direct object marked by the object marker را ('the army' as recipient of the order). بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded / he ordered'. تا introduces the subordinate/jussive clause 'so that / that', and هرکسی is the indefinite subject 'everyone / whoever' who is to act.
segments.s489-g8: بسازند is the subjunctive/jussive 3rd plural 'they should build' (the action commanded by بفرمود). کشتی و زورق بسی lists the objects 'ships and small boats', with بسی ('plenty/enough') indicating a large number; word order places verb before its objects in this poetic line.
segments.s489-g49: ز (a poetic form of از) introduces a list of reasons/qualities — here it means 'by/for/on account of'. The three nouns are linked by و as a simple coordinate list: گنج (treasure/wealth), بزرگی (greatness, grandeur), شایستگی (worthiness). Each noun names a quality or source of merit being ascribed to the person.
segments.s489-g50: The same preposition ز repeats to introduce further qualities: ز آهستگی (from/for his آهستگی). هم placed after the first noun-group means 'also'/'as well', linking the next phrase: ز بایستگی. آهستگی here carries the evaluative sense 'gentleness/mildness' rather than mere slowness, and بایستگی means 'what is fitting/obligation/propriety'.
segments.s489-g51: به introduces the spheres in which he excels: به رای 'in counsel/plan' and به گفتار نیکی 'in good speech' (گفتار modified by نیکی = 'good speech'). These parallel prepositional phrases qualify the following noun گمان, which here functions as 'one's thought/expectation' or the idea people form about him.
segments.s489-g52: نبینی is the negative present (second-person/indefinite) of 'دیدن' used generically: 'you do not see' / 'one does not see'. به مانند = 'like', so به مانند او = 'like him'. در جهان is the locative 'in the world'. Together: 'you will not see like him in the world.'
segments.s489-g53: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' (someone/a certain one). شارستان 'city, fortified town' is the object; کرده is the past participle 'made/built' and دارد is an auxiliary meaning 'has' (کرده دارد = 'has made/has built'). ز + سنگ indicates material 'of stone'.
segments.s489-g54: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' referring back to the town; نبساید is negative of بساییدن meaning 'to avail/serve' (here: 'will not help/avail'). آن هم = 'that too' or 'even that' (هم taking the 'even' nuance), and ز چنگ پلنگ is an ablative/agent phrase 'from the claw/paw of the leopard' (چنگ = claw, پلنگ = leopard), i.e. vulnerable to destruction by the leopard.
segments.s489-g55: زمین here refers to the tract of land/ground. چار فرسنگ is a measure phrase 'four farsangs' (چار = 'four', فرسنگ = 'farsang/parasang') and بالای اوی means 'over/above/of him/it' (بالای + اوی (his/its)), giving the extent around/above the place. Word order puts the measured quantity after زمین with بالای + possessive to indicate extent relative to 'him/it'.
segments.s489-g56: برین is the fused form of بر + این ('on/over/with respect to this'); هم = 'also/too'. نشانست is a stative verb 'it is a sign/mark', and پهنای اوی = 'his/its breadth' (پهنای = breadth, اوی = his). Together: 'Moreover, this is also a sign of his breadth.'
segments.s489-g33: نگارنده (the agent) + بشنید (past) states that the painter heard. زو here is the poetic/conjunctive 'زو' (from/thereupon) linking the hearing to the next action; بر‌نشست is the past verb composed of the verbal root نشست with the preverbal particle بر indicating an upward or immediate motion, so together it means 'thereupon sat up' or 'rose/sat up'. Word order is literary: verb follows subject and the small particles (زو, بر) modify the verb.
segments.s489-g34: به فرمان مهتر میان را ببست is a prepositional phrase plus verb: به فرمان مهتر = 'by/at the command of the chief/headman' (به here = 'by' or 'at the order of'), میان را is the definite/direct-object-marked noun 'the assembly/middle/hall', and ببست is the past-of بستن 'he shut/closed'. The subject remains implicit (the same actor from the preceding clause) and the را marks میان as the object of ببست.
segments.s489-g35: به مصر آمد = 'he came to Egypt' (آمد past of آمدن with the destination marked by به). از اندلس = 'from al‑Andalus' (postverbal source phrase). چون نوند uses چون = 'like/as' and نوند is a proper/musical term (نَوَند) used adverbially here to indicate manner ('in the fashion or mode of Navand'). The sequence is compact and typical of poetic word order (source phrase follows the verb).
segments.s489-g36: بر قیصر اسکندر ارجمند is a prepositional phrase: بر here functions as 'before/in the presence of' or 'toward' + قیصر اسکندر = 'Caesar Alexander' with the postpositive adjective ارجمند 'esteemed/noble.' Word order: preposition + title + name + adjective.
segments.s489-g37: This is a correlative 'چه ... چه ...' construction meaning 'whether ... or ...': چه برگاه دیدش = 'whether (he) saw him at the royal court' (برگاه = royal platform/court; دیدش is the past verb 'saw' with the enclitic object -ش 'him'). چه بر پشت زین = 'or (saw him) on the back of the saddle' (بر = on/upon, پشت زین = the back of the saddle/when on horseback).
segments.s489-g38: بیاورد is past 'he brought' or 'he produced.' قرطاس و دیبای چین is a coordination: قرطاس = 'paper/sheet' and دیبای چین = 'brocade of China' (دیبا = brocade/silk; چین here 'China' forming a genitival phrase). The verb governs the noun phrase as its object.
segments.s489-g39: نگار سکندر = 'the painting/portrait of Alexander' (نگار as a noun 'picture/painting' with the following name in genitive). چنان هم که بود = 'just/precisely as he was' (چنان هم = 'just so/thus'; که بود = relative clause 'that he was' — بود is past 'was'). The clause states the likeness was faithful to reality.
segments.s489-g40: نگارید is a past/passive form meaning 'was painted' (classical literary verb form used here for the act of painting), followed by و linking the next action. ز جای = 'from the place' (ز = from, جای = place), برگشت = 'returned' (past), and زود = 'soon/quickly' (adverb modifying برگشت). The clause says the painter left and returned quickly.
segments.s489-g25: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’. لشکر سواری is a compound where لشکر (host, army) is followed by سواری (mounted men / horsemen). مصور is a postpositive adjective meaning ‘painted/ornamented/depicted’. بجست is the simple past (3rd sg.) of بَجَستن, here ‘sprang/leapt forth’. The whole segment = “from the host of horsemen sprang an ornamented rider.”
segments.s489-g26: که introduces a relative clause ‘who/that…’. مانند is a preposition/comparator ‘like’ and صورت is ‘form/appearance’ as the object of مانند. نگارد here functions as a verb in the relative clause meaning ‘keeps/represents/portrays’ (archaic/poetic present), and درست is an adverb ‘exactly/properly’. Literally: ‘who kept/held the likeness exactly like the appearance.’
segments.s489-g27: بدو is the dative/clitic-like phrase ‘to him’ (to that person) placed before the verb. گفت is the past verb ‘said’ (3rd sg.); since the provided lemma senses did not include the verbal sense ‘said’, that sense is marked as unavailable. سوی is the preposition ‘toward’, followed by the proper name سکندر. خرام is an epithet/adjective meaning ‘striding / with a proud gait’, describing Alexander’s manner as he approaches.
segments.s489-g28: وزین modifies مرز: وزین is an adjective ‘weighty / dignified / grave’, and مرز here means ‘frontier / boundary’ (used figuratively as ‘dignified domain’). و simply connects clauses. از ما مبر is a negative imperative phrase: از = ‘from’, ما = ‘us’, مبر = negative imperative ‘do not take’ (from بردن). هیچ is an indefinite determiner ‘any/none’, and نام is ‘name / reputation’. The command means roughly ‘preserve this dignity/frontier and take no name/reputation from us’ (i.e., do not claim our name/reputation).
segments.s489-g29: به ژرفی is an adverbial phrase ‘deeply / with depth’ (به + ژرفی). نگه کن is the imperative ‘look / watch’ (نگه = gaze/watch, کن = do). چنان چون که هست is a comparative/colloquial cluster meaning ‘just as / exactly as it is’ (چنان and چون reinforce one another; که هست = ‘that is’).
segments.s489-g30: به کردار is an adverbial phrase ‘in conduct / in action / in practice’ (به + کردار). تا introduces a temporal/purpose clause ‘until / so that’. چون here functions as ‘when / as’, and برآیدت is the impersonal/3rd‑person-root form برآمدن with the enclitic ـت (‘to you’): ‘becomes possible / comes about for you / your hand can reach’. دست is literal ‘hand’ and in this idiom means ‘ability / reach / power’. The clause: ‘act so that when the time comes your hand can reach it.’
segments.s489-g31: This is a three‑fold listing introduced by repeated ز ‘from/of’. رنگ here means complexion/appearance (coloring), چهر means face/appearance, and بالای اوی means ‘his height / his stature / the height above him’ (بالای = ‘height/above’, اوی = possessive ‘his’). The repeated ز lists attributes to be used for making a likeness.
segments.s489-g32: یکی is the indefinite numeral ‘one / a single’. صورت آر is a verb phrase where صورت = ‘likeness / form’ is the object and آر is the verb ‘to adorn / to make’ (آراستن) in an imperative/infinitival sense here = ‘make / fashion’. از سر پای اوی is the idiomatic range ‘from head to foot of him’ (از + سر + پای + possessive اوی). The whole: ‘Make a single likeness of him from head to foot.’

sentences.s489-s29: If you ask the treasure, it has no measure; his words are nothing new in the world.
sentences.s489-s9: Alexander became glad at seeing him; that same one said, "May he become an ill-wisher (toward him)."
sentences.s489-s10: The king remained inside Egypt for one year, so that the king and the army might have rest.
sentences.s489-s11: There was a woman who was a ruler in Andalus, wise and with an innumerable army.
sentences.s489-s12: Qeydafeh, the ambitious and generous one, was (there); from a fair face he had gained desire and profit.
sentences.s489-s5: Jahān-gir, with an army on the march, set his face from Jeddah toward Egypt.
sentences.s489-s6: In Egypt's interior there was the sovereign Qeytun; his host, by appearance on the road, was far greater.
sentences.s489-s7: When he heard, Jahān‑gir the Victorious came from the sanctuary along the road, with wind and breath (i.e. swiftly).
sentences.s489-s8: He was received with a plentiful host; (they received him) with moon-like beauties, captives, a crown and rich stores.
sentences.s489-s21: When Qeydāfeh saw Alexander's face, sorrow came upon her; she sat down and drew a breath at the doorway.
sentences.s489-s22: Alexander asked Qeytun and said, 'Who on earth is Qeydāfeh's match?'
sentences.s489-s23: To him Qeytun said, 'O king, there is no one so successful in the world.'
sentences.s489-s24: No one knows the number of his army, unless someone again searches the records.
sentences.s489-s1: When he returned and came to the palace court, he bestowed several dinars on Nasr.
sentences.s489-s2: He who had been a dervish became wealthy; and if it fell to him by effort, it was by his own striving.
sentences.s489-s3: And from that place the joyful army set out; they entered Jeddah, and not much remained.
sentences.s489-s4: He commanded the army that everyone should build many ships and boats.
sentences.s489-s25: For his treasure, his greatness, and his worthiness; for his gentleness as well, and for his propriety.
sentences.s489-s26: In counsel and in good speech, you will not find his equal in the world.
sentences.s489-s27: A certain town has been made of stone, but it will not avail — even that is at the mercy of the leopard's claw.
sentences.s489-s28: The land measures four farsangs around it; moreover, that too is a sign of his breadth.
sentences.s489-s17: The painter heard it and thereupon sat up; at the headman's command he closed the assembly.
sentences.s489-s18: He came to Egypt from al‑Andalus — like Navand — before the noble Caesar Alexander.
sentences.s489-s19: Whether he saw him at the royal court or on the back of a saddle, he brought paper and Chinese brocade.
sentences.s489-s20: Alexander's portrait was painted just as he was, and the painter quickly returned from the place.
sentences.s489-s13: From the host of horsemen sprang a painted/ornamented rider, whose appearance matched the likeness exactly.
sentences.s489-s14: He said to him as he strode toward Alexander: “Preserve this dignified frontier, and take no name from us.”
sentences.s489-s15: Look deeply, just as it is; act accordingly until your hand can reach (it).
sentences.s489-s16: From his color, from his face, and from his height, fashion one likeness of him from head to foot.
